panelReasoning: All three judges agree the stem describes a generic/product-line (off-the-shelf) product - software architected so the same capability serves many later customers - and that this option is simply absent: A ('Customer-specific program') is its exact antonym, B ('Program files') is not a taxonomy term, and C ('Program generator') is a code-emitting tool, not a reusable product. The raw bundle carries the same three options, so the fourth option was lost at source and no listed choice is correct.

The stem is only 'given a figure, what does it represent?' and the figure does not exist (flagged missing_figure, no file in public/figures/). The four options differ solely in edge-vs-level triggering and clock polarity, which are readable only from the symbol's clock input (bubble/triangle), so the stem carries zero discriminating information and any pick is a 1-in-4 guess.

Two judges independently de-watermarked the figure and read the decoder tree as X (root) -> Z -> Y, i.e. significance order XZY, which matches the figure's own handwritten choice (d) - but the stored options are YXZ / ZXY / ZYX / XYZ and contain no XZY, so the option set does not match the figure's and no letter can be keyed. The third judge additionally found the sketch's decoder pins and Y1..Y3 outputs unlabelled and the stem incoherent (a 3x8 decoder has 8 outputs); the item must be rebuilt, not answered.

Two judges key B (Consistency): IEEE 830 consistency is precisely the absence of conflict between requirements, so 'taking into account competing requirements' is its subject matter, while Feasibility (buildable within constraints) and Maintainability (of delivered software, not the requirement set) are off-topic. The third judge keys D (Stability - modification without unexpected effects) at 0.3, and all three note the true textbook term ('modifiable' / requirements negotiation) is not offered, so the majority answer stands but the stem is garbled and the key is disputed.

The figure is missing, and the stem alone makes several options simultaneously true: a circled group yielding a full 4-literal product ABCD is a single cell, hence literally a minterm (A), and if isolated it is also a prime implicant (C) and the sole cover of its own minterm, i.e. essential (D). Two judges reach D but only at 0.5-0.6 with the item flagged multi-true, and the skeptic holds it unresolvable without seeing whether the cell is isolated - the options must be repaired before it can be scored.

All three judges enumerated the transcribed wiring (I0=A^B, I1=B, I2=I3=B', selects C,D) and got the same 8-minterm function, F = B'C + A'BC' + AB'D' + BC'D, which is not a nameable operation (no adder, comparator or majority). The mux figure is missing, so neither the data-pin order nor which of C/D is the select MSB can be verified - and swapping the select order yields a different function - so a short-answer item asking for 'the operation' has no verifiable answer.

The item is 'given a truth table, what is the function?' with the truth table entirely absent (flagged missing_figure) and only SR vs JK offered; those two differ in exactly one row (11 -> toggle for JK, forbidden for SR), which is precisely the information the missing table carried. The circumstantial lean toward JK from sibling items is cross-item pattern-matching, not a derivation, and the surviving sibling table is itself corrupt - so answering would be a coin flip.

Two judges (including the skeptic) key B (JK): the printed twin knpc2018-q004 shows the same question with its input columns intact - 00 hold, 01 reset, 10 set, 11 toggle - and its answer page reads 'JK Flip Flop'; T is excluded (single input) and SR would print an invalid/X row rather than a Q-shaped glyph. The third judge dissents that the only zero-error reading of the degraded cells (Qnext = Q, Q, 0, 1, no complement) is a gated-D flip-flop, an option not offered, so the key rests on assuming a dropped overbar and the figure needs restoring.
